scope: rust-CAN is a software library component. This HARA covers the contribution of rust-CAN to system-level hazards when integrated into a road-vehicle ECU. ASIL classification applies to the rust-CAN software partition only; system-level ASIL may differ after architectural decomposition per ISO 26262-9.

operationalSituations.description: Normal operation — CAN bus active, frames being sent and received by application tasks
Bus initialisation — socket or virtual bus connection being established, no frames yet
Bus shutdown — close() called; subscribers are being drained and deallocated

hazards.description: Malformed CAN frame transmitted to the physical bus (invalid ID, bad DLC, protocol constraint violated)

risk.severityJustification: A malformed frame can corrupt ongoing transmission and may force error-passive state on bus participants. Bus-level disruption in a vehicle can affect secondary safety systems (e.g., stability control) but a single node failure is controllable by the driver — S3 would require loss of vehicle control without driver ability to react.
risk.exposureJustification: CAN bus is active for a significant fraction of the operating time (>1% but not continuous in all modes); typical drive cycle has frequent CAN activity.
risk.controllabilityJustification: Most drivers can react to a transient bus disruption by slowing down; some may not notice a brief disruption in a non-critical secondary system.

mitigations: validate_frame() enforces all RELAY §15.1 constraints before any call to the transport send path
SocketCAN kernel driver provides a second layer of frame validation at the socket level

hazards.description: Safety-critical payload silently corrupted — corruption not detected by the E2E layer

risk.severityJustification: Undetected payload corruption in a safety-critical message (e.g., brake command, steering torque) can lead to loss of vehicle control without opportunity for corrective action.
risk.exposureJustification: E2E protection is used selectively on safety-critical signals; random bit errors on CAN are infrequent but non-zero over the lifetime of the vehicle.
risk.controllabilityJustification: Driver may be able to detect abnormal vehicle behaviour if the error persists but a single corrupted frame may act before the driver can react.

mitigations: CRC-16/CCITT-FALSE over header and payload detects single-bit errors with probability 1
CRC provides Hamming distance ≥ 4 for message lengths up to 32767 bits

hazards.description: Replayed or out-of-order E2E-protected message accepted as authentic

risk.severityJustification: A replayed safety command (e.g., an old brake-release message) could suppress an active safety function at a critical moment.
risk.exposureJustification: Replay attacks require deliberate action; accidental replay can occur if a transmitter restarts and re-sends from counter 0.
risk.controllabilityJustification: Vehicle behaviour may seem normal initially before the effect of the replayed command becomes apparent.

mitigations: 32-bit monotonic sequence counter in the E2E header; Receiver rejects non-consecutive counters
Counter wrap (at 2^32) is a known limitation; applications requiring wrap detection shall reset the Receiver or implement application-level sequence management

hazards.description: Subscriber channel overflow — frames silently dropped without application awareness

risk.severityJustification: Dropped frames on a non-safety CAN channel cause data gaps but do not directly lead to physical harm; safety-critical channels should use the E2E sequence counter to detect gaps.
risk.exposureJustification: Subscriber backlog is possible any time the consumer is slower than the producer.
risk.controllabilityJustification: Application developer can select the appropriate back-pressure policy; end users have no direct control.

hazards.qmRationale: S1 × E4 × C3 maps to QM per ISO 26262-3 Table 4. No safety goal is required. Applications using rust-CAN for safety-critical subscriptions shall use the E2E layer (REQ-SAFETY-005) to detect sequence gaps caused by dropped frames. This is documented in SAFETY_PLAN.md §8 Known Limitations.

hazards.description: ISO-TP receive blocks indefinitely when consecutive frames do not arrive

risk.severityJustification: A permanently blocked receive task can starve other safety-relevant tasks in the same Tokio runtime, causing latency violations in secondary functions.
risk.exposureJustification: Multi-frame ISO-TP receive is used less frequently than single-frame CAN traffic.
risk.controllabilityJustification: A task that blocks indefinitely is fully controllable by the system watchdog or timeout mechanism; the standard ISO-TP timeout is 250 ms.

mitigations: IsoTpConn.recv() wraps all waits in tokio::time::timeout using the configured deadline (default 250 ms)
Error::Timeout is returned to the caller; the Tokio task is released and the runtime is not starved

safetyGoals.description: rust-CAN shall reject all CAN frames that violate RELAY §15.1 protocol constraints before transmission
safetyGoals.safeState: Frame is rejected with Error::InvalidFrame; no transmission occurs on the physical bus

safetyGoals.description: rust-CAN E2E protection shall detect payload corruption, tampering, and replay attacks with probability ≥ 1 − 2^−16
safetyGoals.safeState: Corrupted or replayed data is rejected with E2EError; the payload is not forwarded to the application

safetyGoals.description: rust-CAN shall bound all blocking receive operations by the caller-supplied context deadline (default 250 ms)
safetyGoals.safeState: Error::Timeout is returned; the Tokio task is released and the runtime continues to schedule other tasks
